text: Hi everyone, the video presentation today is a collage of five laparoscopic cholecystectomies done in our unit by surgical trainees. The complexity grading of these operations were marked as easy to moderately difficult. the video will demonstrate the basic principles of doing a safe laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

Familiarity with the anatomy of the hepatobiliary region is vital to doing a safe cholecystectomy procedure. Although the terms the callous triangle and the hepatocystic triangle are used interchangeably, the correct terminology to use is a hepatocystic triangle. That's because in the callus triangle, the cystic artery forms the cephalic boundary instead of the liver surface. This distinction is very important because it is the hepatocystic triangle that needs to be demonstrated and cleared rather than the callus triangle.
The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with a right tilt, and the standard port placement for laparoscopic cholecystectomy is visible, including a camera port and three additional ports.

The surgeon grasps the fundus of the gallbladder and pushes it toward the right shoulder, clearing any fibrofatty connective tissue. Next, the neck of the gallbladder is grasped and pushed down toward the right hip, opening up the hepatocystic triangle to demonstrate the critical view of safety.

The surgeon demonstrates the safety line, which extends from the Rouvier's sulcus to the umbilical fissure at the bottom of liver segment four. The Rouvier's sulcus, visible on the undersurface of the right lobe of the liver, is approximately 2.5 centimeters long. Dissection above this line is considered safe.

A safety line also called the R for U line is described by Gupta et al in a paper which is a must read for all surgeons wanting to do a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. I'll leave a link in the description below. To obtain a satisfactory critical view of safety, three criterion has to be fulfilled. Number one, the hepatocystic triangle has to be cleared of fat and fibrous tissue. Number two, the lowest part of the gallbladder is separated from the cystic plate. Number three, only two structures should be seen entering the gallbladder. These are the cystic artery and cystic duct.

The surgeon clips and divides the cystic artery and cystic duct after achieving the critical view of safety. The neck of the gallbladder is initially pulled upward, causing misalignment with the common bile duct, but is then adjusted to display the critical view properly. The gallbladder is subsequently removed in a routine manner.

Fibrofatty tissue is visible creeping up onto the body of the gallbladder. The surgeon pulls the neck of the gallbladder upward to dissect in the posterior part of the hepatocystic triangle, ensuring the safety line is visible. The dissection is focused on the body of the gallbladder to open the posterior hepatocystic triangle, avoiding blind dissection.

The surgeon encounters small bleeding branches from the cystic artery, creating a messy surgical field. The gallbladder wall appears fibrous and thick, and the fundus is identified as the optimal area for grasping.

The surgeon uses suction to remove the contents of the gallbladder, facilitating the grasping of its fundus. Fibro fatty tissue is removed by pulling the gallbladder upwards, ensuring the line of safety is visible during cephalid dissection. A suction device aids in the dissection process.

The surgeon observes that the cystic duct in this patient is still partially covered with fibro fatty tissue. It is recommended to skeletonize the cystic duct, removing the surrounding tissue, before proceeding with clipping and division.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery located posterior to the fibrofatty tissue within the hepatocystic triangle. Despite the incomplete clearance of fibrofatty structures, the artery is safely clipped using surgical clips.

Last but not least, be very aware of a very short cystic duct. This is in particular difficult to identify when the gallbladder is quite pendulous and bulky. long as the principle of safety are followed which is proper dissection of the hepatocystic triangle and the demonstration of safety line and keeping the dissection above the safety line no harm can be done to the vital structures
I hope you've enjoyed today's video presentation. Please leave your comments below and subscribe to our channel for future similar videos. Many thanks.
